attributes.stem: A 24-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of an itchy scalp with white flakes visible on his dark clothing. He has tried regular washing with normal shampoo without improvement. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are white scales visible on the scalp without evidence of inflammation, excoriation, or hair loss.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of seborrhoeic dermatitis, commonly known as dandruff or pityriasis capitis, which is thought to be caused by overgrowth of the fungus Malassezia species on the scalp. The condition is characterised by white scaling without significant inflammation or hair loss. It also can be present on the face in a 'butterfly' rash pattern, trunk or scrotum as areas of sebaceous glands. Adult seborrhoeic dermatitis is managed differently to paediatric seborrhoeic dermatitis - the later associated with excessive sebaceous gland activity leading to yellow scaley patches early in life.

Selenium sulfide shampoo is one of the first-line treatments for dandruff. It has both antifungal and keratolytic properties, making it effective at controlling Malassezia growth and removing scales. It should be used 2-3 times weekly for 2-4 weeks, then weekly for prevention.

Permethrin cream is used to treat scabies and other ectoparasitic infections. It would not be effective for dandruff, which is caused by fungal overgrowth rather than parasitic infestation.

Topical mild hydrocortisone cream would be difficult to apply to the scalp and is not indicated for seborrhoeic dermatitis. Betamethasone 0.05% or mometasone 0.1% lotion may be required if the antiyeast shampoo alone is ineffective.

Oral fluconazole is excessive for uncomplicated dandruff, which responds well to topical treatments. Systemic antifungals would only be considered in severe, resistant cases of seborrhoeic dermatitis.

Tea tree oil shampoo, while having some antifungal properties, has not been shown to be as effective as selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoos in controlled studies. It may be used as an adjunct treatment but should not be considered first-line therapy.
learningPoints: Selenium sulfide and ketoconazole shampoos are equally effective first-line treatments for dandruff.
Treatment should be continued for 2-4 weeks, then used intermittently for prevention.
references: Dall'Oglio F, Nasca MR, Gerbino C, Micali G. An Overview of the Diagnosis and Management of Seborrheic Dermatitis. Clin Cosmet Investig Dermatol. 2022.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9365318/
Bholah N, Oakley A, Gomez J. Seborrhoeic dermatitis. DermNet NZ. 2024.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/seborrhoeic-dermatitis
